message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 14 (31 todo)
Recent changes: Green extended QuoteItem with optional material/enchantment/cursed, and added per-item surcharge logic. To reach 165 for the cursed sword without breaking the prior plain-item base tests (which assert no surcharge), Green folded BOTH the 50% curse surcharge AND the 10% first-insurance surcharge into the cursed-item branch (i.e. surcharge is 0 for non-cursed items).

This is a known temporary shape: first-insurance genuinely applies to EVERY item per the spec, but the prior "isolated base" tests omit it, so it's currently only attached to cursed items. Upcoming tests (high-enchantment surcharge, first-insurance policy-wide, follow-up discount, loyalty, integration examples) will force this to be lifted into proper item-specific (curse, high-enchantment) and policy-wide/per-item (first-insurance) modifiers.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted. Do NOT prematurely generalize the modifier structure beyond what the 14 passing tests demand, but DO improve naming/clarity if the curse+first-insurance conflation can be made clearer without changing behavior. Do NOT modify existing tests.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 14 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase produced a deliberately misleading conflation. Let me evaluate the key names:

- `itemSurcharges` — Returns surcharges for an item. But the body conflates TWO distinct concepts: a 50% curse surcharge AND a 10% first-insurance surcharge, both gated behind `if (!item.cursed)`. The name `itemSurcharges` does NOT reveal that first-insurance is hiding inside the cursed branch. This is a **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)** problem.

- `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` — This constant is used inside the cursed-only branch, which is semantically wrong (first-insurance applies policy-wide per spec). The name reveals intent at the constant level, but its *placement* misleads.

The most important issue: the cursed branch is doing two unrelated things, and a reader cannot tell from `itemSurcharges` that first-insurance logic is buried there.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on the `itemSurcharges` function (the locus of the conflation):

```
const itemSurcharges = (item: QuoteItem): number => {
  if (!item.cursed) {
    return 0;
  }
  const base = itemBasePremium(item);
  return base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE + base * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
};
```

Component count:
- Constants: `0` (return) = 1
- Bindings: `item`, `base` = 2; plus references to `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`, `item.cursed`, `base`×2, `base`... let me count binding references: `item.cursed`(1), `item`(1 in call), `base`(assigned), `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`(1), `base`(1), `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`(1) = ~6 bindings
- Invocations: `itemBasePremium()`(2), `*`(2)×2=4, `+`(2) = 5 invocations = 10
- Conditionals: `if`(1) = 4
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: `const base =`(1 binding init, counted as binding not mutation) = 0 mutations

Approximate Total Mass ≈ 1 + 6 + 10 + 4 = **21**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 14 green. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** This is where the problem lives. The expression `base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE + base * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` blends two semantically distinct surcharges into one line, and the function name `itemSurcharges` hides that first-insurance is here at all. A reader cannot see the two distinct concepts.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** `base * X` pattern appears twice but with different rates — acceptable, distinct knowledge.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Not over-engineered.

The clearest improvement WITHOUT changing behavior or prematurely generalizing the structure: make the two conflated surcharges *visibly distinct* and *named*, so the temporary curse+first-insurance conflation is self-documenting. I'll extract two clearly-named local concepts and add a brief comment marking the known-temporary placement of first-insurance.

This keeps the same call structure (no premature generalization into policy-wide modifiers) but reveals intent.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

Command "build" not found.

content.text: No build script; let me run the TypeScript compiler directly to confirm no type errors.

Type-check passes with no errors.

## Step 6: Calculate New APP Mass

The behavior is unchanged; the logic is now split across two clearly-named functions plus a composing function.

`curseSurcharge`:
- Bindings: `item.cursed`, `item`, `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `0` ≈ constant 1 + bindings 3
- Invocations: `itemBasePremium()`(2), `*`(2) = 4
- Conditional: ternary(4)
- Subtotal ≈ 1 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 12

`firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly`: same shape ≈ 12

`itemSurcharges` (composer):
- Bindings: `item`×2 = 2
- Invocations: 2 calls(4) + `+`(2) = 6
- Subtotal ≈ 8

Total ≈ **32** (up from ~21).

Mass change: 21 → 32 (Δ +11).

The mass increased because I split one conditional into two and added a composing layer. **Per the project rules, Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP.** The increase buys a critical clarity gain: the two distinct surcharge concepts are now separately named and visible, and the known-temporary placement of first-insurance is explicitly documented. The previous single-line conflation actively hid the first-insurance logic behind a `cursed` gate and a generic `itemSurcharges` name — a genuine intent-revelation defect that this refactoring corrects.

## Step 7: Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring applied:** Decomposed the conflated `itemSurcharges` into two clearly-named functions — `curseSurcharge` and `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` — composed by `itemSurcharges`. Added a comment documenting that first-insurance's cursed-only placement is temporary and spec-wise belongs policy-wide.

**Rules applied:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 14 tests green before and after. ✅
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The two distinct surcharge concepts (50% curse, 10% first-insurance) are now separately named and visible instead of fused into one expression hidden behind a single `cursed` guard. The function name `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` makes the known-temporary shape self-documenting. This is the primary improvement.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): The `item.cursed ? itemBasePremium(item) * RATE : 0` shape repeats, but each instance encodes distinct knowledge (different surcharge, different rate, different future destiny) — extracting a shared helper would obscure that they are conceptually separate. Left intentionally distinct.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Added two functions, but they serve the clear purpose of separating two concepts; not over-engineering.

**Mass change:** ~21 → ~32 (Δ +11). Mass increased, but **Rule 2 trumps APP** per project standards. The clarity gain (exposing and labeling the hidden first-insurance conflation) justifies the higher mass.

**Did NOT prematurely generalize:** The modifier structure was not lifted into policy-wide modifiers. First-insurance remains attached at the item level (cursed-only) exactly as the 14 passing tests demand — the upcoming first-insurance-policy-wide test will force that generalization, not this refactor.

**Tests:** 14 passing, 31 todo. Type-check clean. ✅
